texts.orig: abstract
texts.text: abstract

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactors are mostly dedicated to industrial degassing of liquids, and generally speaking, to gas transfer with fluids. They are usually commercialized under the form of hollow fiber membrane contactors. They also receive some attention for the extraction or the absorption of contaminants from aqueous solutions or gas.  Thus, they become a useful tool to solve environmental issues. This text mostly dedicated to industrial users will illustrate this interest by surveying three applications namely: gas absorption from air or flue gas (liquid-gas system), gas removal from aqueous solutions using trans-membrane chemical absorption (liquid-gas-liquid system) and removal of solubilized ions from water (liquidliquid system). These options cover almost all the environmental uses of this technology, which is particularly simple and convenient when the contamination sources are not permanent or/and when small volumes are involved. However, contactors can be utilized for a wider range of situations and flow rates as they can be easily tailored to any size.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrane contactors were developed on an industrial scale in the 1990s for the degassing of industrial fluids to  reduce  corrosion  due  to  dissolved  gas. They  are commercialized under the form of hollow fiber membrane contactors  and  are  almost  exclusively  available  from LiquiCEL ® , today a division of 3 M (Liquicel, 2016).  They are still globally used for degassing industrial liquids, where they have successfully replaced columns, towers or other venting extractors for nearly 20 years. They are also in demand in water deoxygenation, water decarbonisation and  carbonation  processes  in  the  food  and  beverage industry.  A  membrane  contactor  is  a  system  which allows the contact between the water and a gas through a  hydrophobic  membrane. Water  circulates  from  one side of the membrane and the gas on the opposite wall (Gabelman and Hwang, 1999; Kentish and Stevens, 2001; Stanojevi  et al.,  2003; Klaassen et al., 2005; Bringras et al., 2009; Drioli et al., 2011; Parhi, 2013; Pabby et al., 2015). Typically  the  pore  sizes  are  in  the  range  of  30-50  nm and the membrane is made of polypropylene, physically treated to enhance its hydrophobic character.  They are able to remove oxygen to the ppb level if the fluid is stored in  a  tank  blanketed  with  nitrogen.  Figure  1  shows  the experimental set-up used for degassing. Compared to steam and/or chemicals degassing, this simple process brings  significant  savings.  For  instance,  with  chemical treatment,  10  ppm  of  sodium  sulfite  is  needed  to remove one ppm of oxygen; contactors do not require any chemicals. They can easily handle hundreds of cubic meters per hour. Gas displacement may also be performed by using a sweep (strip) gas such as nitrogen instead of (or in combination with) vacuum.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Since  the  late  1990s,  this  technology  has  been  very popular for liquid-liquid extraction and reactive liquid-liquid extraction  (also  known  as  reactive  solvent  extraction (RSE)). As a reminder RSE is commonly used in industry to extract organic and inorganic products. In such a process, the solute present in an aqueous solution is extracted into an immiscible solvent containing a specific extracting (carrier) agent. When these experiments are performed in conventional dispersion-based contacting devices such as column or mixer-settlers, two problems may arise during this operation. Firstly, the extracting agent (usually a toxic or environmentally harmful compound) and the solvent are sligthly dispersed into the aqueous medium and later in the environment. Secondly, upon phase mixing, generally long lasting (sometimes permanent) emulsions are commonly observed. The use of hollow fiber contactors introduced a  microporous  membrane  which  provides  a  physical partition between the feed and the solvent streams. The solvent phase circulates on one side of the hydrophobic fiber  which  saturates. The  aqueous  feed  flows  on  the opposite side where a low trans-membrane pressure is applied to maintain the organic phase at the pore mouth of the membrane. This pressure must be below the break through  pressure  to  prevent  water  from  crossing  the membrane wall. As a result, the organic phase being not dispersed, emulsions are totally eliminated. For the same reasons,  water  contamination  from  the  organic  phase is considerably reduced. It is important to note that the membrane maintains  a  large  and  constant  surface  of exchange between the two media. Therefore, it is easier to predict the performances of an extraction and this eases its scaling-up. The water phase may circulate within the fiber (lumen side) or outside (shell side) (and vice versa for the organic phase).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Contactors  also  received  attention  for  emulsion  liquid membrane extractions (ELM). An ELM process consists of an external (or feed) phase (containing the contaminant to  be  extracted),  an  organic  membrane  phase  and  an internal phase (stripping or receiving phase). The internal phase and the membrane is a w/o emulsion, dispersed into the external phase, in which the extraction process occurs. Its advantage over RSE is that the direct and the back extraction of the contaminants are carried out at the same time. Therefore, the extraction process is more efficient,  but  it  is  more  tricky  to  operate  (see  note  1). Studies  combining  ELM  -  hollow  fiber  contactors  are not new, as metal extraction (copper) has already been reported  in  1998  (Hu  and  Wiencek,  1998).  Generally speaking,  performing  ELM  in  hollow  fiber  contactor reduces significantly the solvent and carrier (extractant) requirements (Fouad and Bart, 2008).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: In the early 2000, the idea to circulate two aqueous phases on both sides of the hydrophobic membrane came out to treat water streams contaminated by volatile compounds such as NH 3 , CO 2 , H 2 S, etc.  These compounds may diffuse across the membrane wall if a proper aqueous extracting phase is selected on the other side of the membrane. The dissolved gas is stripped from the contaminated phase because trans-membrane chemisorption occurs. There is  no  risk  of  mixing,  the  two  fluids  being  water-based solutions, they are not able to cross the membrane wall. This technique is popular for NH 3 abatement, a big issue in water treatment that, so far, has not received satisfactory answers. This particular case will be presented in more details later on in this text.

texts.orig: Most studies on contactors, whatever the applications, use  polypropylene  (PP)  hollow  fiber  module  from Liqui-Cel. Other types of contactors exist using either other fiber material like Polyvinylidene difluoride (PVDF) or  Polytetrafluoroethylene  (PTFE)  (Tan  et  al.,  2006; Rajabzadeh et al., 2009; Mansourizadeh and Ismail, 2011) or  flat  sheet  membranes  (FSM).  FSM  contactors  are generally made of PVDF or PTFE (Kentish and Stevens, 2001; Stanojevi  et al.  2003;  Hasanoglu  et  al.,  2010). Bougie  et  al.  2015  compared  performances  between flat sheet membrane contactors made of PTFE to those made of PP . PTFE and PP are hightly hydrophobic and their  contact angles with water are comparable. They are respectively equal to 108 and 112. To compare to other  materials,  PVDF  contact  angle  is  only  66.  For hydrophylic  polymers  like  modified  polyacrilonitrile such  as  Gabelman  et  Hwang,  1999; Klaassen and Jansen, 2001; Stanojevi  et al., 2003; Klaassen et al., 2005; Klaassen et al., 2008; Bringas et al., 2009, Drioli et al., 2011; Parhi, 2013; Pabby et al., 2015 will  not  be  systematically  mentioned unless  when  they  represent  the  main source of information.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Figure 2 shows the commonly encountered experimental set-up used for trial tests. This system may be assembled or dismantled within few hours. The authors (and most of the other authors) performed trial extractions using a 2.5X8 Liquid-Cel ®  hollow fiber contactor bought from Liquid-Cel (Charlotte, NC, uSA). It is made of polypropylene hollow fibers with a 0.05 micron pore size and a surface area of 1.4 square meters. Typically, the lumen and shell sides are feed with two independent pumps: gear ( Case i ) or peristaltic ( Case ii ) pumps depending on the applications and/or the corrosive properties of fluids. With this set-up, flow rates up to 60 L/h are easily reached.

texts.orig: This configuration is used to reduce organic or/and inorganic wastes. In this situation, the extracting phase is organic, circulates inside the fiber and comes out to the outer side of  the  membrane  wall. Waste  water  flows  on  the  shell side (however, the phase feeding order may be inverted). The organic phase is currently made of a light solvent like heptane and extracting agents. These latter are generally phosphorus or sulfurous based compounds such as the well-known Cyanex derivatives from Cytec Industries Inc. (Woodland Park, NJ, uSA; Cytec, 2016) which became part of the Solvay group. Nitrogen based compounds such as Alamine 336 and Aliquat 336 (Sigma-Aldrich, Oakville, On., Canada)  are  also  current  extractants. texts.orig: Molybdates,  which  have  been  widely  used  as  an anticorrosion agent in process water, are today prohibited in many countries. Millions of cubic meters of process water  containing  MoO 4 2-   have  to  be  treated  without modifying its quality and its basic pH. So far, only expensive and  poorly  efficient  ionic  exchange  resins  are  able  to address  this  problem,  otherwise,  pH  and/or  chemical adjustments are required, spoiling these neat H 2 O stocks. Basualto et al., 2003, successfully extracted MoO 4 2-  from acidic solution by using Alamine 336 in kerosene carried out in a polytetrafluoroethylene flat sheet contactor and performing Mo(VI) back extraction with sodium carbonate. Early  work  from  Sato  et  al.,  1989,  already  reported extraction of molybdenum with hollow fiber modules using phosphonic acid derivatives in acidic medium. At basic pH, molybdenum is under its MoO 4 2-   form and only Aliquat 336 is suitable for its direct extraction at this pH (Galan et al., 1994). RSE of Mo(VI) with Aliquat 336 in solution in aromatic solvents at basic pH has been conducted by the authors on a laboratory scale in a separatory funnel. Although efficient, after mixing, phase separation occurs very slowly and the solubility of aromatic solvents is not negligible (Total Organic Carbon (TOC) was ca. 400 ppm in the aqueous phase), leaving a high organic content in the treated water. Samples of process water containing 250-150 ppm of MoO 4 2-  have been treated by an organic phase made from a 0.1 M Aliquat 336 in solution into xylenes  on  a  hollow  fiber  contactor  already  described and shown in Figure 2. The extractant circulated inside the  fiber  and  the  water  outside.  A  pressure  gradient (34 kPa) was maintained across the membrane in order to  keep the organic phase inside the fiber. texts.label: text
texts.orig: Ammonia is widely  spread  into  wastewaters  because of the decomposition of organic matter, agricultural and industrial activities. Due to its high marine toxicity and its nutrient role on aquatic flora, its treatment has become a priority. But, this latter problem is difficult to solve because the existing technologies are either too cumbersome or expensive as they have not been ideally designed for these applications. There are many ways to remove ammonia from a liquid, but the most popular is air stripping (see for instance Branch Environmental Corporation, 2016).  This standard and well-known process is a two steps operation. Firstly,  the  contaminated  water  flowing  at  a  basic  pH (which must be adjusted before injection in the stripper) is distributed over the internal packing of a column while air enters at its bottom from a blower. Air travels upwards, stripping ammonia and carrying the mixture to a second column where the ammonia must be either thermally destroyed into nitrogen and water or simply chemically neutralized by an acidic solution cascading in a washing column. Ammonia destruction is seldom performed by thermal treatment because it involves a high initial and operating cost, but it does not generate secondary wastes. This technique is selected only for large installations. The usual alternative is the most common and simple acid neutralization (normally sulfuric acid) which, unfortunately, produces a huge volume of acid waste difficult to valorize, even if ammonium sulfate may be sold as a fertilizer. The washing solution, once spent, cannot be regenerated. This  bottle-neck,  added  to  the  large  size  of  columns and air-consumption, discourages most of the potential users.  Other  techniques  like  biological  treatments  can be considered, but they have also limitations and are not always easy to run.  The well-known Anaerobic Ammonium Oxidation (Anammox) process (Paques, Balk, Netherlands; Paques, 2016) is a good example. Anammox bacteria are able  to  oxidize  ammonium  (electron  donor)  directly  to gaseous nitrogen under anaerobic condition where nitrite plays the role of an electron acceptor. However Anammox bacteria  are  slow  growing  in  nature  with  a  generation time of approximately two weeks and their growth can be suppressed by denitrifying biomass (Du et al., 2014; Abbasi et al., 2014). texts.label: text
texts.orig: On the other hand, contactors may offer a good alternative for treating ammonia by using chemical stripping instead of air. In aqueous wastes, ammonia is present under its soluble NH 4 +   form (except if the pH of the solutions is far larger than 9.25). At low pH, no stripping may occur because NH 4 +   is  too stable. Therefore, the pH must be increased in order to create conditions for a chemically induced  ammonia  stripping  by  converting  ammonium ions to soluble and volatile ammonia. The NH 3 /NH 4 +  pKa being 9.25 at 25 ° C, the pH must be larger than this value because ammonia should become the dominant specie into the solution. A pH value of 12 is recommended to warranty a good result. Once this is done, the solution can be fed into the contactor, but before doing so, a 10 micron filtration is recommended to preserve the module from clogging. Filtering is applied after pH adjustment, because for most applications when the pH is raised, precipitation occurs. When the NH 3 rich  solution  flows inside the module, water is not able to cross the pores of the microporous hollow fiber walls due to the hydrophobic nature of the membrane. Only ammonia is able to do so. On the other side of the membrane, an acid solution at a low pH circulates and quickly absorbs NH 3 as long as a  pH gradient is maintained between the two phases. This parameter is the driving force of the exchange, it is recommended to operate at pH 2 and sulfuric acid is commonly used due to its low cost.

In aqueous wastes, ammonia is present under its soluble NH 4 +   form (except if the pH of the solutions is far larger than 9.25). At low pH, no stripping may occur because NH 4 +   is  too stable. Therefore, the pH must be increased in order to create conditions for a chemically induced  ammonia  stripping  by  converting  ammonium ions to soluble and volatile ammonia. The NH 3 /NH 4 +  pKa being 9.25 at 25 ° C, the pH must be larger than this value because ammonia should become the dominant specie into the solution. A pH value of 12 is recommended to warranty a good result. Once this is done, the solution can be fed into the contactor, but before doing so, a 10 micron filtration is recommended to preserve the module from clogging. Filtering is applied after pH adjustment, because for most applications when the pH is raised, precipitation occurs. When the NH 3 rich  solution  flows inside the module, water is not able to cross the pores of the microporous hollow fiber walls due to the hydrophobic nature of the membrane. Only ammonia is able to do so. On the other side of the membrane, an acid solution at a low pH circulates and quickly absorbs NH 3 as long as a  pH gradient is maintained between the two phases. This parameter is the driving force of the exchange, it is recommended to operate at pH 2 and sulfuric acid is commonly used due to its low cost.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The recent on-line review on trans-membrane ammonia extraction  from  Kunz  and  Mukhtar  (2016)  is  a  good starting point for those not familiar with the technique. This  article  includes  detailed  ammonia  chemistry  and contactor  principles.  Experimental  and  theoretical analysis of ammonia trans-membrane absorption has also been investigated in details by Hasanoglu et al., 2010, using  sulfuric  acid. This  reference  describes  ammonia absorption using hollow fiber and flat sheet membranes. The Ashrafizadeh and Khorasani's work limited to hollow fiber  contactor,  also  described  in  details  the  influence of  the  main  parameters  on  the  ammonia  exchange (Ashrafizadeh and Khorasani, 2010). Agrahari et al., 2012, also covered NH 3 extraction in hollow fiber contactor and demonstrated that the separation rate is independent of the initial ammonia concentration in the range (220-1500 ppm). Lauterböck et al., 2012, and Wäeger-Baumann and Fuchs, 2012, also published data using this technology for the removal of ammonia from anaerobic digestion reactor and its effluent. The on-line Segura's thesis (Segura, 2012) is also very instructive. Liqui-Cel application notes show the diagram and the expected performances of the pilot plant built by this company (Liquicel, 2016a).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Membrana  GmbH  (Wuppertal,  Germany)  in  2002  has done  the  first  pioneering  work  on  a  significant  scale. The company installed a pilot plant to test the removal of  ammonia for  its  own  waste  utilizing  two  Liqui-Cel ® contactors in series. Two technical notes describing this effort  are  available  from  Liqui-Cel  web  site  (Liquicel, 2016a). The  installation  was  able  to  treat  0.5-1  m³/hr of waste water and, in 2004, the plant was upgrade to operate up to 30 m³/hr. In addition to this landmark in 2002, reviews by Stanojevi  et al., 2003; Klaassen and Jansen, 2001; Klaassen et al. 2005 & 2008 covered this particular application. BLuE-tec Process Water Technology (Renkum The Netherlands, BLuE-tec, 2016) also offers units for membrane ammonia stripping using sulfuric acid.

The  installation  was  able  to  treat  0.5-1  m³/hr of waste water and, in 2004, the plant was upgrade to operate up to 30 m³/hr. In addition to this landmark in 2002, reviews by Stanojevi  et al., 2003; Klaassen and Jansen, 2001; Klaassen et al. 2005 & 2008 covered this particular application. BLuE-tec Process Water Technology (Renkum The Netherlands, BLuE-tec, 2016) also offers units for membrane ammonia stripping using sulfuric acid.

texts.orig: In  the  authors'laboratories,  phosphoric  acid  has  been selected  instead  of  conventional  sulfuric  acid,  even  if it is less efficient and more expensive, because it leads almost directly to monoammonium phosphate (MAP) and/ or diammonium phosphate (DAP), the two most popular fertilizers. Nitric Acid might be an option, as it will conduct to a solution of NH 4 NO 3 , also an important fertilizer difficult to handle in a solid state due to its high hygroscopy and its explosive properties. Figure 2 shows the experimental set-up used for these trial testings ( Case ii ). The lumen and shell sides are feed with two independent peristaltic pumps at a flow rate varying from 0.5 L/min to 2.0 L/min. In this work, the acid circulates outside the hollow fibers (shell side) and the NH 3 rich solution flows in its inner side (lumen side). If ammonia is diluted, the dead volume of the contactor on the shell side being larger, it is better to circulate the acid inside the fiber. Figure 4 illustrates the importance of the choice of the acid and its concentration on the trans-membrane absorption of ammonia. In this experiment, the ammonia concentration is large and can be taken as a constant during the extraction (400 L of 1 g/L ammonia solution at pH ca. 11.00) compared to phosphoric acid (4 L conc. 0.33 M). Therefore, the exchange is only governed by the acid gradient. It is seen that the rate of exchange is linear as long as the pH is below ca. 7 and after it is significantly reduced and quickly stopped, even if  almost  half  of  the  acid  neutralization  capacity  is  still available. Therefore, phosphoric acid is interesting only if there is an end-user for this waste, otherwise a stronger acid will be a better choice.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: From all the results published in the litterature, it is shown that  whatever  the  initial  concentration  of  ammonia, experiments have demonstrated that almost a 98% NH 3 removal is assured in most cases. It works better if the ammonia concentration is larger than 150 ppm. For lower concentrations, the trans-membrane exchange becomes slow and this technique may not represent the best option, except  if  the  treated  volumes  are  small.  In  this  case, batch operation in a closed loop mode can be selected, but the treatment time is extended. The main advantage of this technique is to be suitable for a large number of applications and is especially convenient for effluents in small quantities (because the technology is compact and simple to handle). They are able to cancel two important disturbing facts while compared to air stripping: no air is  required  and,  generally  speaking,  a  sharp  reduction of the process size is observed. Even so, industries are reluctant to adopt this technique due to a lack of history in this particular field. This technique for ammonia treatment has rougthly only 20 years of existence. They also express two main concerns about the technology: the membrane integrity and there is no gain on the cost of chemicals while  compared  to  stripping,  a  process  that  is  more trusted by the industrial community.

texts.orig: The first concern is important. The membrane must be kept hydrophobic otherwise the two liquids get mixed up. This may happen with solutions containing a high organic content, tensioactive agents or/and sticky molecules. In this situation, it may be difficult to keep the membrane integrity. The authors test these modules with a mixture of ammonia solubilized into commercial milk, a perfect fouling agent, to measure their resistance. They did resist for several days (14) on a continuous mode. Biodigestates, pig manures, photographic solutions, mine lixiviates have also been tested on a shorter time scale and the modules did not lose their hydrophobicity. However, to overcome this potential intrinsic disadvantage, the authors suggest to use flat sheet membrane contactors instead of hollow fiber contactors when sample cleanliness is an issue. One has been home-built using PTFE laminated membrane (pore size 220 nm) obtained from Sterlitech Corporation (Bellevue, WA, uSA). PTFE and PP hydrophobicity degrees

texts.orig: are almost similar, except that laminated PTFE sheets has a far better mechanical resistance compared to PP flat sheets. The membrane supported by a plastic grid has been  sandwiched  between  two  pipe  flanges  and  this simple set-up creates two ca. 700 mL compartments that can be mounted in series like a press filter (see Figure 5). Table  1  compares  the  result  obtained  with  the  two geometries. Flat membrane contactors take more space, but if the membranes loss its hydrophobic property it can be easily washed or replaced.  Typically, the packing density for  hollow  fiber  module  is  between  1000-1200  m 2 /m 3 compared to 100-300 m 2 /m 3  for flat sheet configurations. However, hollow fiber contactors, when damaged, can not be repaired and this bothered most of the potential users, their replacement being a non-negligible expense.

texts.orig: From Table 1, in term of NH 3 exchange, the flat membranes are more efficient for the same surface, because their pore size is larger, but their breakthrough pressure range is smaller. This might be a problem for large flow as the internal  pressure  will  increase.  For  dirty  samples,  the authors  are  also  developing  immerged  bags  made  by squeezing the same PTFE membranes (220 nm pore size) with a PP frame. The bags are connected to a pump via polypropylene tubing and support the extracting solution which are directly soaked into the water tank to be treated. This  works like  dialysis  units  and  are  not  subjected  to fouling. This idea is similar to the one exploited by Majd et al., 2012, who used tubular gas-permeable membrane to reduce ammonia from animal manures directly from the tank. Wäeger-Baumann and Fuchs, 2012, use an identical approach by submerging the contactor in a bio-reactor without its casing.

texts.orig: Another point, related to the membrane integrity, is its ability to prevent ionic species or unwanted contaminants to diffuse across the membrane. The authors verify this hypothesis and the membrane seems an efficient chemical barrier.  It  blocks  most  of  the  chemicals.  For  instance, no significant chemical diffusion through the membrane was observed after its exposition to ammonia solutions contaminated by salt (up to 36 g/L) to mimic seawater or by sugar (up to 10 g/L) to imitate organic matter. Similarly, no significant migration of acid is observed towards the basic side. Table 2 shows examples of data collected (the experimental conditions are indicated in the legend).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: tried to take advantages of the presence of phosphate to  precipítate  an  agronomically  useful  product  on  the spot,  technically  known  as  struvite  (NH 4 MgPO 4 .6H 2 O). Struvite precipitation is a competent way of phosphorus removal and recovery from different sources with higher phosphorus concentrations (>10 mg/L) (Pratt et al., 2012). If the chemistry of this reaction is simple, the precipitation is not trivial. It requires an excess of at least one of the reagents, the pH of the solutions must be kept above 9 and the medium must be maintained at a temperature around  40 ° C  for  better  results  (Rahman  et  al.,  2014), followed by addition of magnesium salts and concurrent pH adjustment by NaOH or Mg(OH) 2 (Le Corre et al., 2009). An efficient struvite precipitation is difficult to achieve and, today, the company Ostara (Ostara, 2016) is among the few able to produce it on a large scale from wastewater. Its patented process has been designed for nutrient recovery, especially phosphorus in wastewaters, which is eliminated by  combination  with  ammonia  already  present  in  the aqueous wastes. The operation is performed in a fluidized bed reactor and the resulting struvite is sold under the commercial name Crystal Green ® . The process might be used for ammonia abatement but it has not been designed for this application (see comments in note 3). Also, the manufacture of MAP directly on the spot seems, so far, one of the best avenues (see comments in notes 4 and 5). However, whatever the ideas, it is difficult to valorize the acid wastes with either strippers or contactors.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Another  drawback  is  the  requirement  to  increase  the pH of the water phase in order to be able to strip NH 3 . Also, the direct ammonium removal from concentrated aqueous  solutions  using  contactors  and  an  extracting solution (Hossain and Chaalal, 2016) has been proposed in order to skip this step. In their work, ammonia is extracted under  its  highly  soluble  form  NH 4 +   by  ionic  exchange with the proton of D2EHPA (di-(2-ethylhexyl) phosphate) molecule  solubilized  in  sunflower  oil. They  obtained ammonia removal between 43-73%. By selecting this approach instead of the trans-membrane gas absorption seen previously, it becomes useless to increase the pH of the solution.  This represents a tremendous advantage and will introduce an interesting short-cut if the recipe can be improved. This skips the regular purchase of chemicals (ie. NaOH) and the need of a mixer-settler, the pH adjustment being not required. This also eliminates the production of  muds (usually low-value metal hydroxides) regularly observed after this operation.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The  increase  in  CO 2 emissions  and  its  resulting greenhouse effect affect our climate and this question should  be  addressed.  Carbon  capture  and  storage technologies exist. CO 2 removal is generally performed in bubble columns, packed towers, scrubbers, etc., but these processes are bulky and complex to operate. Contactors, widely  used  in  the  food  industry  for  carbonation, represent an interesting method for CO 2 reduction from any type of emission sources. Whatever the size and/ or the concentration of the CO 2 streams, efficient liquid absorption is observed. Generally, the gas to be treated is fed inside the hollow fibers and the absorbent on the other side. Kumar et al., 2002, mentioned that the absorbent selection  is  important.  Reactive  absorption  must  be efficient;  otherwise the extraction yield is limited. This eliminates pure water as absorbent, because after CO 2 absorption its pH decreases, and this reduces the CO 2 mass transfer. Traditionally, the industrial CO 2 absorbents are  mineral  bases  such  as  Ca(OH) 2 ,  NaOH  or  organic bases (amines or/and their derivatives). Kumar et al., 2002; D' Alessandro et al., 2010; Yu et al., 2012 and Ouellet et al., 2015 have reviewed the various techniques of CO 2 capture by absorption and adsorption. These references covered a wide range of techniques and absorbents. The present manuscript will give only a bird-eye view of this topic. When mineral based are used, the absorption process is not reversible and the end product is a carbonate salt which has little commercial value. Ca(OH) 2 is  banned in contactors, its carbonate being insoluble, the contactors will be quickly damaged. When CO 2 is  present in large amount, amines absorption is preferred as CO 2 absorption is reversible. However, the desorption step requires a large amount of energy and the CO 2 release must be treated by other techniques (generally it is liquefied by compression if present in large quantities). Witek-Krowiak et al., 2012, has studied in detail the behavior of CO 2 absorption using amines  based  absorbents  in  contactors.  Zhang  et  al., 2014, published a well documented review including the perspectives for the treatment of flue gas with contactors and the text is conveniently available from the web. Zhang et al., 2006, studied the properties of single and blended alkaloamines solutions in hollow fiber modules. Yan et al.,  2007 ,  investigated  the  separation  of  CO 2 from  flue gas also using hollow fiber contactors feed with amines and potassium glycinate. Yeon et al., 2005, built a PVDF contactor pilot plant and show that the CO 2 absorption rate  per  unit  volume  of  the  membrane  contactor  was 2.7 times larger than that of the packed column. Yu et al., 2012a, used aqueous ammonia solutions for CO 2 capture with contactors, an unusual option, but additives must be added to promote the CO 2 absorption rate which is too low with aqueous ammonia alone. Therefore, as it is seen CO 2 capture using the combination of conventional absorbents

Therefore, as it is seen CO 2 capture using the combination of conventional absorbents

texts.label: text
texts.orig: During the last decade, the traditional absorbents tend to be replaced by ionic liquids ( iLs ) to lead to a greener chemistry.  The introduction of iLs has opened another field of research. In fact, CO 2 is readily soluble in iLs (Albo et al.,  2010; Zhang et al., 2011; Lu et al., 2014). Generally, these iLs are quaternary ammonium salts or imidazoles. The solubility of CO 2 depends on many factors the nature of  the iLs ,  its  counter-ion,  its  molecular  weight,  the temperature, the CO 2 partial pressure, etc. (Ramdin et al., 2012; see note 6). This is beyond the scope of this text, but CO 2 solubility is well covered in the extensive reviews by Ramdin et al., 2012, Torralba-Calleja et al., 2013, Lei et al., 2014 and more recently, Li et al., 2016. One advantage of  this  type  of  absorbents  over  the  commonly  used monoethanol amine (MEA) is the enthalpy released during this operation. It is around 200-300 Kj/Kg CO 2 ,  80-90% less than for MEA, making its back-extraction easier.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Once CO 2 is solubilized in iLs , further processing may be carried out depending on the iLs selected. In fact, after CO 2 absorption, this latter might be chemically combined with  the iLs and  leads  to  more  valuable  compounds. Therefore, this synthesis gives birth to products of interest and, more important, CO 2 ends up permanently trapped in a molecule. In other words, when CO 2 is absorbed in ionic liquids and furthermore chemically converted, these steps are similar to photosynthesis. This is seducing, as photosynthesis is the best way to reduce CO 2 footprint. This sequence is usually carried out in separate reactors: one for CO 2 absorption, and a second one for the synthesis, the experimental conditions required being far different for the two steps. For instance, Cheng et al., 2012, used high pressure (15 MPa) and temperature close to 110 ° C in order to add CO 2 molecule on selected iLs . Generally cyclic carbonates have been derived using this synthetic pathway ( iLs + CO 2 ) (Albo et al., 2010; Cheng et al., 2012; Liu et al., 2012; Ouellet et al., 2015). These molecules are important as they are intermediates for the production of electrolytes for lithium batteries and green solvents (Zhang et al., 2011). They are also encountered in the synthetic routes of atorvastatin, a compound that helps cholesterol reduction (Rádl et al., 2002). Today, iLs have been already explored in contactors. Tylman and Snochowska, 2014, gave an overview of their applications and the advantages brought by the combination contactor-ionic liquids (this reference is not only dedicated to CO 2 capture).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: However,  when  it  is  time  to  develop  large  industrial applications, iLs present  two  major  drawbacks. They remain  still  costly  but  it  is  believed  that  their  prices may drop if they are sold (produced) in larger quantities (Ramdin et al., 2012; see note 6). The second drawback, more limiting for contactors, derives from their viscosity. iLs exhibit  high  viscosity,  up  to  several  hundred  times than that of water and upon the physical absorption of CO 2 , an increase is commonly observed even if it involves low energy interactions. Thus, to pump them inside the module becomes a tedious task and it gets worst upon CO 2 capture. In addition, this also reduces the efficiency of  the  gas-liquid  exchange.  This  fact  seems  related to  the  absorption  mechanism.  It  is  believed  that,  CO 2 occupies  the  voids  within  the iLs structure  through  a large quadrupole moment and Van der Waals interactions (Ramdin et al., 2012; Torralba-Calleja et al., 2013).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To overcome these disadvantages (cost and viscosity), the  authors  have  tested old  fashioned iLs such  as phosphate or amino based extractants currently found in hydrometallurgy. In term of cost there are at least 50 to 100 times cheaper than iLs . Their viscosities are high (20150 mPa.s), but they are solubilized at a low concentration in a solvent such as heptane (0.39 mPa.s). The authors selected  Aliquat  336,  a  quaternary  ammonium  salt and Alamine 336, a tertiary amine and did perform trial extractions  using  0.05-0.50  M  Aliquat/Alamine  336  in heptane in a 2.5X8 Liquid-Cel ®  hollow fiber contactor as shown and described before. As a reminder, the cartridge is  made of PP fibers with a 0.05 micron pore size and has a surface area of 1.4 square meters. The CO 2 rich gas was fed inside the fiber (lumen side) and the absorbent liquid was circulating outside the fiber (shell side). These expriments have realized with standard absorbents such as monoethanolamine (MEA), sodium hydroxide (NaOH) and with Aliquat 336 and Alamine 336 solutions. From the absorption data recorded, one realizes that among all the products tested, MEA remains the most efficient. Therefore, if  MEA absorption efficiency is set to unity (taken as the reference), the comparison of their relative absorption efficiencies for similar experimental conditions, are respectively as it follows: MEA (1.00 by definition)/ NaOH (0.67)/Aliquat 336 (ca. 0.23)/Alamine 336 (ca. 0.26). The absorption-desorption cycles are reversible and do not require energy for Aliquat 336 and Alamine 336. These ionic liquids can be only used as a reversible absorbent, because they do not have the properties to form chemical bond with CO 2 .  Therefore, they are not suitable for further organic synthesis. Although not as efficient as MEA, all the process is done at room temperature, their price is very low and there are no viscosity problems.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The purpose of this manuscript was to give a quick survey of the use of contactors in environmental science. They are particularly convenient when space is limited because they are smaller than conventional devices used for the applications cited. For trial tests, pilot modules can be quickly assembled and operate within few hours. They are a perfect fit to treat small volumes. In addition, no high pressure is required; this limits fouling and complex pumping systems. So far, they did not receive a wide audience in the industry whatever the importance of the problems to solve. This is probably due to the fact that the  technical  risks  are  still  not  well-documented,  this process lacking history (less than 20 years). In addition, contactor reported applications are mostly using hollow fiber modules, which, when damaged, cannot be repaired and must be replaced.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note 2: Anammox process does away with aeration and external carbon source requirements, thus it is cost effective as compared to energy consuming and space demanding conventional nitrificationdenitrification approach (Du et al., 2014). However, partial nitrification is  required to convert ammonium to nitrite and the same can be realized either by controlling pH or temperature or aeration depending on the types of Anammox microbes involved. DO (dissolved oxygen) level of approximately 1 .5 mg/L is required for oxidation of ammonium to  nitrite  and  controling  DO  level  is  a  major  challenge.  Likewise, low organic carbon can completely inhibit the Anammox process. In addition, Anammox bacteria are slow growing in nature with a generation time of approximately two weeks and their growth can be suppressed by denitrifying biomass (Du et al., 2014; Abbassi et al., 2014).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note 3: Struvite  is  applied  as  a  fertilizer  for  the  growth  of  maize and wheat and the enhanced growth of the crops after application, confirmed its role as a fertilizer. In fact, struvite is used as a slow release  fertilizer  due  to  its  slow  nutrient  release  nature  and  low solubility  (0.23  g/L)  as  compared  to  commercial  slow  release phosphate  fertilizers  (18  g/L)  (Le  Corre  et  al.,  2009). Therefore, it  is  more  valuable  than  traditional  fertilizers,  offering  number  of advantages  like  (i)-  prolonged  nutrient  delivery,  (ii)-  minimizing frequency of application, (iii)- diminishing harmful effects associated with over dosing of fertilizers, etc., (Bhardwaj et al., 2012; Rout et al.,  2016). Struvite precipitation can be realized by both biological and chemical means, much of the published researches focused on chemical precipitation whereas a very little research was carried out on biological precipitation (Pratt et al., 2012; Rout et al., 2014; Rout et al., 2015).  T oday, the Ostara process is among the few able to produce struvite  on  a  large  scale. This  is  mostly  dedicated  for  phosphate removal on municipal used water taking advantage of the phosphate and ammonia already present in the waste. After this treatment, 90% of P is removed under the form of struvite and 40% of N is left.

texts.orig: Note 5: For  instance,  one  may  be  tempted  to  perform  a  direct degassing from solutions contaminated by NH 3 by using vacuum stripping instead of trans-membrane absorption, as it is done for oxygen degassing on water streams (see Figure 1).  This is expected to work if ammonium ions have been previously converted to ammonia NH 3 by increasing the pH of the solution (and/or its temperature). Practically, it is impossible to strip ammonia using vacuum because its Henry constant is too large (59 M/atm compares to 3.4.10 -2  M/ atm for CO 2 or 6.2.10 -4  M/atm for N 2 ).  The advantage of this approach would have been to obtain NH 3 stripping without the aid of air or chemical solutions. In addition, one would have obtained a gas stream containing only ammonia that can be condensed by cryogeny and/or compression and/or absorbed on a solid medium.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Concerning NH 3 capture by solid absorption, the work of the Danish company Amminex in this field deserves few lines. In 2005, they developed a unique way of storing and transporting gaseous ammonia using a solid magnesium chloride derivative that was able to capture six molecules of NH 3 per mole of MgCl 2 (Amminex, 2016). After solid absorption, NH 3 could be released by thermal desorption and this produced a concentrated ammonia stream that can be more easily treated than a diluted source of NH 3 . More recently, they improve the recipe and propose to substitute magnesium chloride by strontium chloride. This compound can store 450 g of NH 3 in  a  compressed block of one liter volume. SrCl 2 is able to trap eight NH 3 molecules and the system is reversible by thermal treatment. At 80 ° C, the solid looses seven NH 3 molecules and, if heats up to 150 ° C, the last NH 3 molecule is released. Although this idea is actually exploited for NO x reduction in exhaust pipes of diesel engine, it might be a seducing system to bypass acid absorption in strippers. In fact, after air stripping, the ammonia flow might be sent to a column filled with SrCl 2 instead of being washed by an acid. This option, if it works, might jeopardize the benefit of using contactors!

texts.orig: Note 6 :  For example, the Difasol process launched by the French Petroleum Institute (IFP) (Total, Paris, France) which employs ionic liquids for the production of isooctane from butene on a large scale, demonstrates that it  might  be  economically  viable. The  BASIL™ process (BASF , Ludwigshafen, Germany), which is dedicated to the production of diethoxyphenylphosphine, a photoinitiator prepared by  reaction  of  dichlorophenylphosphine  with  ethanol  is  another successful industrial example. In this application, 1-methylimidazole was used as an acid scavenger insead of tertiary amines that were too problematic.
texts.text: Note 6 :  For example, the Difasol process launched by the French Petroleum Institute (IFP) (Total, Paris, France) which employs ionic liquids for the production of isooctane from butene on a large scale, demonstrates that it  might  be  economically  viable. The  BASIL™ process (BASF , Ludwigshafen, Germany), which is dedicated to the production of diethoxyphenylphosphine, a photoinitiator prepared by  reaction  of  dichlorophenylphosphine  with  ethanol  is  another successful industrial example. In this application, 1-methylimidazole was used as an acid scavenger insead of tertiary amines that were too problematic.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Note 7 : CO 2 -triggered switchable solvents (CTSS) is a new avenue in CO 2 capture. These compounds have been introduced by Jessop and al. in 2005. Recently Park and al., 2015, reviewed their interest in CO 2 capture. These authors provided a review of the potential of various anhydrous solvents for CO 2 capture (not only switchable solvents). However, as mentioned by this research team, their goal was not to provide a complete coverage of the existing literature, but rather to pinpoint the major findings in this field. These CTSS are becoming very popular as they are probed as draw solutions in forward osmosis (Wilson et al., 2013). Their low production cost compared to other ionic liquids adds attractiveness.
